summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes using sleep biosignals as a general pretraining distribution for adjacent biosignal domains. By employing multimodal contrastive pretraining on 432,000 hours of sleep data, the authors show that the resulting representations transfer to eight non-sleep EEG and ECG tasks. Results indicate that sleep pretraining consistently outperforms training from scratch and is competitive with domain-specific foundation models on several EEG benchmarks.

headline_results: Consistently outperforms training from scratch
Competitive with LUNA and LaBraM on TUAB, TUSL, and CHB-MIT
Maintains 95% performance with 16-50% EEG channels

key_points: New EEG foundation model approach: leverages large-scale sleep PSG data for pretraining to improve performance on non-sleep biosignal tasks.
Achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results on EEG benchmarks like TUAB, TUSL, and CHB-MIT, outperforming some much larger models.
Demonstrates high channel robustness, maintaining 95% performance with as few as 16-50% of available EEG channels.
limitations: Transfer evaluated only on modalities proximal to PSG (EEG and ECG).
Mixed validation protocols across different datasets make direct cross-task statistical comparison difficult.
Limited baseline comparisons for the TUSL dataset.
ECG transfer gains were smaller than EEG gains due to sparser cardiac signals in PSG.

method.finetuning: Full fine-tuning with a lightweight projector/classifier head.
method.pretraining: Pretrained on 432,000 hours of sleep data using 1s patches and 2m windows.

summary.one_liner: Demonstrates that pretraining on large-scale sleep polysomnography data effectively transfers to non-sleep EEG and ECG clinical tasks.
summary.title: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks
summary.unique_contribution: Establishes that sleep-only PSG pretraining provides a highly transferable representation for wake-state clinical EEG and ECG tasks, even without domain overlap.

papers.title: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks

reasons: Explicitly references sleep foundation models and the goal of learning representations that transfer to adjacent domains.
Evaluates the transferability of pretrained representations to non-sleep EEG tasks.
Focuses on the effectiveness of pretraining distributions for reusable biosignal models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces NeuralBench, a standardized framework designed to evaluate AI models of brain activity across multiple modalities, including EEG, MEG, and fMRI. NeuralBench-EEG v1.0 evaluates 14 architectures across 36 tasks and 94 datasets, utilizing a shared training recipe to ensure fair comparison between foundation and task-specific models. The results indicate that current EEG foundation models only marginally outperform task-specific baselines and that cognitive decoding tasks remain significantly challenging.

headline_results: Foundation models only marginally outperform task-specific models
REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA are top performers

key_points: Benchmark framework: NeuralBench provides a standardized pipeline (NeuralFetch, NeuralSet, NeuralTrain) for evaluating NeuroAI models across EEG, MEG, and fMRI.
Large-scale EEG evaluation: NeuralBench-EEG v1.0 tests 14 models on 36 tasks and 94 datasets, finding that foundation models offer only marginal gains over task-specific ones.
Task difficulty insights: Identifies cognitive decoding and clinical predictions as high-difficulty areas where current models still struggle.
limitations: Currently restricted to decoding models, excluding encoding, forecasting, and denoising tasks.
Relies primarily on end-to-end finetuning, omitting parameter-efficient strategies like LoRA.
Some foundation models were pretrained on data that overlaps with downstream benchmark datasets.
Uses fixed splitting strategies rather than comprehensive cross-validation for all tasks.
Preprocessing pipelines are inherited from original model papers rather than systematically optimized.

method.architecture: Framework consisting of NeuralFetch (data access), NeuralSet (preprocessing), and NeuralTrain (modular training).
method.finetuning: Standardized recipe: AdamW optimizer, 10^-4 learning rate, cosine annealing, and a linear projection head.

summary.one_liner: A unified, open-source framework for benchmarking NeuroAI models across various brain recording modalities, starting with a large-scale EEG benchmark.
summary.title: NeuralBench: A Unifying Framework to Benchmark NeuroAI Models
summary.unique_contribution: Provides a unified, modality-agnostic benchmarking framework and a large-scale EEG evaluation suite (NeuralBench-EEG v1.0) with 36 tasks and 94 datasets.

papers.title: NeuralBench: A Unifying Framework to Benchmark NeuroAI Models

reasons: Introduces NeuralBench-EEG v1.0, a large-scale benchmark specifically for EEG AI models.
Explicitly evaluates and compares current foundation models against task-specific models.
Fits the ecosystem criteria as a systematic benchmarking framework for EEG-FMs.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes CORTEG, a framework that repurposes a pretrained scalp-EEG foundation model (ST-EEGFormer) for ECoG decoding using a KNNSoftFourier spatial adapter and a dual-stream tokenizer for low-frequency and high-gamma activity. Unlike prior subject-specific ECoG decoders, CORTEG enables cross-patient learning and rapid calibration via a leave-one-subject-out fine-tuning strategy with LoRA. Evaluation on finger trajectory and audio envelope regression tasks shows that CORTEG matches or exceeds state-of-the-art task-specific baselines while requiring significantly less per-patient data.

headline_results: Finger trajectory: r=0.554 (pooled), beating DeepFingerNet (0.542)
Audio envelope: r=0.339 (pooled), beating CNN-LSTM (0.261)

key_points: New EEG-to-ECoG transfer framework: Adapts pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models to intracranial recordings using a coordinate-aware spatial adapter and dual-stream tokenization.
Efficient cross-subject deployment: Employs a two-stage leave-one-subject-out fine-tuning strategy with LoRA, allowing new patient calibration in 10-30 minutes.
Strong empirical performance: Achieves state-of-the-art mean correlation on finger trajectory (r=0.554) and audio envelope (r=0.339) regression tasks.
limitations: Reliance on MNI-space localization which does not fully capture subject-specific functional anatomy.
Lack of cross-task transfer evaluation between different ECoG tasks.
No observed scaling law with increasing model size, possibly due to limited per-patient data.
Simple mean-pooling fusion of low-frequency and high-gamma streams may be suboptimal.

method.architecture: ST-EEGFormer backbone with a KNNSoftFourier spatial adapter and a dual-stream tokenizer (LFS 1-64Hz, HGA 70-200Hz).
method.finetuning: Two-stage Leave-One-Subject-Out Fine-Tuning (LOO-FT) using LoRA on the final transformer blocks.
method.pretraining: Masked autoencoding on scalp EEG (via ST-EEGFormer).

summary.one_liner: A cross-modality transfer framework that adapts pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models to intracranial ECoG for data-efficient BCI decoding.
summary.title: CORTEG: Foundation Models Enable Cross-Modality Representation Transfer from Scalp to Intracranial Brain Recordings
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a cross-modality transfer framework that enables pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models to be adapted to intracranial ECoG recordings for high-performance, data-efficient BCI decoding.

papers.title: CORTEG: Foundation Models Enable Cross-Modality Representation Transfer from Scalp to Intracranial Brain Recordings

reasons: Explicitly utilizes and adapts 'pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models (EEG FMs)' as the core backbone.
Focuses on the adaptation and cross-modality transfer of EEG-FM representations to ECoG.
Proposes a specific framework (CORTEG) for fine-tuning and repurposing EEG foundation models.

summary.detailed_summary: CLEF proposes a two-stage framework that shifts EEG foundation modeling from short-window BCI decoding to session-scale patient representation. It first employs a VQGAN to tokenize multi-channel multitaper spectrograms and pretrains a ViT encoder via masked reconstruction. In the second stage, the model aligns these patient-level embeddings with LLM-summarized neurologist reports and structured EHR data using symmetric contrastive objectives. Evaluation on a new 234-task benchmark using over 260k sessions shows that CLEF significantly outperforms prior foundation models, improving mean AUROC from 0.65 to 0.74.

headline_results: Mean AUROC improved from 0.65 to 0.74
Outperformed baselines on 229/234 tasks

key_points: New EEG foundation model: CLEF learns patient-level representations from full-session recordings rather than short segments.
Combines masked reconstruction of multi-channel spectrogram tokens with contrastive alignment to LLM-summarized clinical reports and EHR data.
Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on 229 of 234 clinical tasks, improving mean AUROC from 0.65 to 0.74.
limitations: Lack of real-time support
Context window insufficient for full sleep architecture or multi-day trends
Limited interpretability of embeddings

method.architecture: Two-stage pipeline: VQGAN tokenizer and ViT Encoder for spectrograms, followed by a contrastive alignment stage with T5 and EHR Transformer.
method.pretraining: Stage 1: Masked Image Modeling on spectrogram tokens. Stage 2: Contrastive alignment with clinical text and EHR.

summary.one_liner: A long-context EEG foundation model that aligns full-session representations with clinical reports and EHR data to learn patient-level clinical semantics.
summary.title: CLEF: EEG Foundation Model for Learning Clinical Semantics
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a session-scale EEG foundation model that aligns long-context neural representations with neurologist reports and structured EHR data to capture clinical semantics.

papers.title: CLEF: EEG Foundation Model for Learning Clinical Semantics

reasons: Explicitly identifies as an 'EEG foundation model' (CLEF) designed for long-context clinical EEG.
Presents a pretrained model evaluated on a broad 234-task benchmark, demonstrating generalizable representations.
Utilizes large-scale pretraining on 260k EEG sessions to create reusable representations for clinical semantics.

summary.detailed_summary: TRACE proposes an autoregressive pre-training framework that predicts future EEG patches using a Temporal Routing MoE (TR-MoE) architecture. Unlike standard token-wise MoE, TRACE employs a TemporalFormer router to apply a shared expert decision to all channels at each time step, preserving instantaneous cross-channel coherence. The model was pre-trained on a heterogeneous corpus of over 1.5 million segments and demonstrated state-of-the-art or competitive performance across eight downstream BCI and clinical benchmarks.

headline_results: SOTA on BCIC2020-3 (imagined speech) and FACED (emotion recognition).
Competitive performance on motor imagery and clinical event classification.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: TRACE utilizes an autoregressive multi-horizon forecasting objective to learn causal representations from heterogeneous EEG data.
Novel TR-MoE architecture: Employs a TemporalFormer router to ensure all channels at a given time step share the same expert, preventing the fragmentation of multi-channel coherence.
Strong transferability: Achieves leading results on benchmarks such as BCIC2020-3 and FACED, generalizing effectively to both seen and completely unseen datasets.
limitations: Evaluation is primarily focused on classification-oriented BCI and clinical tasks, lacking validation on regression-style prediction.
Pre-training corpus may not cover all possible acquisition hardware, rare neurological conditions, or artifact patterns.
Increased model and training complexity due to the MoE architecture and expert pool.
Does not address clinical interpretability, privacy-preserving training, or demographic fairness.

method.architecture: Time-frequency patch encoder, multi-scale channel positional encoding, and TR-MoE blocks combining causal spatial-temporal attention with a Cross-Channel Temporal Routing FFN.
method.finetuning: Lightweight 3-layer MLP head for end-to-end fine-tuning on downstream tasks.
method.objective: Multi-horizon autoregressive forecasting (predicting next 1, 2, and 4 patches) using Huber loss and an expert-balancing auxiliary loss.
method.pretraining: Heterogeneous multi-source pre-training on over 1.5 million EEG segments.

summary.one_liner: An autoregressive EEG foundation model using temporal routing MoE to preserve cross-channel coherence while adapting to non-stationary brain states.
summary.title: TRACE: Temporal Routing with Autoregressive Cross-channel Experts for EEG Representation Learning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a cross-channel temporal routing mechanism that applies shared expert decisions to all channels at each time step to maintain spatial coherence during autoregressive pre-training.

papers.title: TRACE: Temporal Routing with Autoregressive Cross-channel Experts for EEG Representation Learning

reasons: Proposes an autoregressive EEG pre-training framework specifically for learning transferable representations.
Designed for heterogeneous pre-training across diverse corpora with varying channel counts, montages, and recording domains.
Evaluated on eight downstream benchmarks, including datasets completely unseen during pre-training, demonstrating foundation-model-style transfer.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a three-stage interpretability pipeline—layer-wise ridge probing, LEACE-style subspace erasure, and transparent closure classifiers—to analyze the representations of CSBrain, CBraMod, and LaBraM. It reveals that while these models broadly encode a 63-feature clinical lexicon, only 68.6% of those features are representation-causal for task decisions. Results demonstrate that confirmed features recover an average of 79.3% of the foundation models' performance advantage over random baselines, with a clear task gradient from high recovery in MDD to lower recovery in stress detection.

headline_results: 89.6% of features encoded
68.6% of features representation-causal
79.3% average recovery of FM advantage

key_points: Benchmark analysis of EEG foundation models: Audits CSBrain, CBraMod, and LaBraM across five clinical tasks using a 63-feature hand-crafted lexicon.
Causal distinction: Finds that 89.6% of features are encoded, but only 68.6% are representation-causal, highlighting a gap between representation and usage.
Performance recovery: Confirmed features recover 79.3% of the FM advantage on average, though harder tasks like stress detection leave a significant unexplained residual.
limitations: Linearity of both the probe and the eraser restricts analysis to linearly decodable components.
The eraser is a cross-covariance approximation rather than the full closed-form LEACE operator.
Several lexicon entries are GPU-friendly proxies rather than exact textbook estimators.
Findings are limited to the three specific models and five tasks tested.

method.architecture: Audit pipeline consisting of layer-wise ridge probing, LEACE-style cross-covariance subspace erasure, and transparent closure classifiers.

summary.one_liner: A systematic causal audit of three EEG foundation models across five clinical tasks to determine the extent to which they rely on established hand-crafted clinical features.
summary.title: What Do EEG Foundation Models Capture from Human Brain Signals?
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a causal auditing framework using subspace erasure to distinguish between what EEG foundation models merely encode versus what they actually use for clinical predictions.

papers.title: What Do EEG Foundation Models Capture from Human Brain Signals?

reasons: Explicitly analyzes and audits three specific EEG foundation models: CSBrain, CBraMod, and LaBraM.
Focuses on understanding the representations learned by EEG-FMs via self-supervised pretraining.
Fits the ecosystem criteria as a systematic evaluation/analysis of EEG-FM capabilities across multiple clinical tasks.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes the use of attention-aware LRP as a post-hoc attribution method to explain the opaque decision-making of Transformer-based EEG foundation models. Unlike previous LRP applications focused on CNNs, this approach targets the Transformer architectures of models like LaBraM to verify biological plausibility and detect spurious correlations. Evidence from R-peak detection, motor imagery, and affect prediction demonstrates that LRP can identify 'Clever Hans' shortcut learning and surface novel sensorimotor hypotheses.

headline_results: R-Peak: ~75% BAC
PhysioMI L/R: >70% BAC in some runs
AffectiveVR: ~55% BAC

key_points: Method for EEG-FM interpretability: Employs attention-aware LRP to generate high-resolution attribution heatmaps for Transformer-based EEG models.
Detection of shortcut learning: Unmasked 'Clever Hans' behavior in motor imagery tasks where models relied on ocular artifacts rather than neural motor correlates.
Scientific discovery potential: Identified a recurring central electrode cluster signature for arousal prediction, suggesting a novel sensorimotor hypothesis.
limitations: Interpretation of EEG heatmaps is difficult without domain expertise and susceptible to subjective bias.
The choice of LRP was based on simulations of non-Transformer models and lacks direct faithfulness validation on EEG-FMs.
Discovery potential is limited by the current performance gap where specialized CNNs still outperform finetuned FMs.

method.architecture: LaBraM (Temporal conv blocks followed by a Transformer encoder)
method.finetuning: Standard finetuning, frozen feature extractor, and training from scratch
method.pretraining: Pretrained on 2500 hours of EEG data

summary.one_liner: Applies attention-aware Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) to interpret the decision processes of Transformer-based EEG foundation models.
summary.title: From Clever Hans to Scientific Discovery: Interpreting EEG Foundational Transformers with LRP
summary.unique_contribution: Extends Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) to Transformer-based EEG foundation models to enable verification of biological plausibility and detection of spurious shortcut learning.

papers.title: From Clever Hans to Scientific Discovery: Interpreting EEG Foundational Transformers with LRP

reasons: Explicitly targets 'EEG Foundational Transformers' and 'EEG-FMs' in both title and abstract.
Focuses on the interpretability and verification of EEG-FMs using Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP).
Fits the ecosystem criteria as a tool for the evaluation and exploration of EEG foundation models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes KAST-BAR, a framework that employs a Dual-Stream Hierarchical Attention (DSHA) encoder to capture the brain's non-Euclidean 3D manifold through bidirectional local-global interactions. It introduces a Knowledge-Anchored Semantic Profiler (KASP) to synthesize instance-level medical profiles using a frozen LLM, which then guide a Semantic Text-Aware Refiner (STAR) to dynamically aggregate EEG features using latent expert queries. Pre-trained on 21 diverse datasets, the model demonstrates superior generalization across six downstream tasks, effectively bridging the gap between low-level physiological signals and high-level textual semantics.

headline_results: KAST-BAR-Large (2.2B) consistently outperformed THD-BAR-Huge and NeuroLM-XL across all six tasks.
Achieved 71.2% B-Acc on Workload and 70.8% B-Acc on TUEV.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: KAST-BAR integrates a dual-stream hierarchical encoder with an LLM-driven semantic profiler to align brain signals with expert medical knowledge.
Dynamic feature aggregation: The STAR module uses latent expert queries to adaptively retrieve task-relevant physiological patterns based on synthesized semantic priors.
Strong empirical results: Pre-trained on 21 datasets, the 2.2B parameter model outperforms existing multi-task models like THD-BAR and NeuroLM across six benchmarks.
limitations: Potential smoothing of microscopic frequency details during quantization, affecting performance on tasks like slowing detection.
High computational complexity due to the billion-parameter LLM backbone.
Inference latency introduced by the autoregressive generation of the KASP module.
Risk of LLM hallucinations in rare pathological scenarios where general-purpose medical knowledge may lack precision.

method.architecture: DSHA encoder (bidirectional global/local streams) + VQ-VAE tokenizer + KASP (frozen LLM profiler) + STAR (query-based refiner) + Qwen2.5 BAR backbone.
method.finetuning: Multi-task instruction tuning using a decoupled LoRA strategy for the BAR backbone and full-parameter fine-tuning for the STAR module.
method.objective: Two-stage objective: (1) VQ reconstruction loss for the tokenizer; (2) Next Token Prediction (NTP) for joint text-EEG autoregressive pre-training plus an expert orthogonality loss.
method.pretraining: Two-stage process: self-supervised VQ reconstruction followed by joint autoregressive pre-training on a corpus of 21 diverse EEG datasets.

summary.one_liner: An EEG foundation model that aligns multi-level brain topology with expert-level medical semantics via a dynamic query-based refinement mechanism.
summary.title: KAST-BAR: Knowledge-Anchored Semantically-Dynamic Topology Brain Autoregressive Modeling for Universal Neural Interpretation
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a 'Topological Encoding - Knowledge Anchoring - Guided Refinement' paradigm that uses LLM-generated medical priors to dynamically steer the aggregation of brain-topology-aware EEG representations.

papers.title: KAST-BAR: Knowledge-Anchored Semantically-Dynamic Topology Brain Autoregressive Modeling for Universal Neural Interpretation

reasons: Explicitly states the goal of building an EEG foundation model
Utilizes large-scale pre-training on 21 diverse datasets
Demonstrates broad transferability across six downstream tasks for universal neural decoding

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a cross-architecture pipeline to audit the internal representations of three EEG transformers (SleepFM, REVE, and LaBraM) using TopK Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). It introduces a clinical semanticity taxonomy and a 'target vs. off-target' probe area metric to quantify concept steering selectivity, distinguishing between surgical feature removal and 'wrecking-ball' interventions. The framework utilizes a spectral decoder to map latent manipulations back to physiologically interpretable frequency signatures, such as the restoration of alpha-band power.

headline_results: SAE reconstruction preserves AUROC within 0.017
Amplitude R2 between 0.772 and 0.816

key_points: Method for mechanistic interpretability: Employs TopK Sparse Autoencoders to extract sparse, monosemantic feature dictionaries from frozen EEG foundation models.
Selectivity quantification: Introduces a probe-based metric to distinguish between selective concept manipulation and 'wrecking-ball' interventions that collapse global model performance.
Physiological grounding: Uses a spectral decoder to translate latent interventions into interpretable frequency signatures, such as restoring alpha-band power.
limitations: Spectral decoder cannot reconstruct phase, preventing time-domain waveform generation.
Observational monosemanticity (separability) does not guarantee interventional selectivity.
Concept taxonomy is investigator-defined and limited by available cohort metadata.

method.architecture: TopK Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) applied to frozen transformer encoders (SleepFM, REVE, LaBraM).
method.objective: MSE on normalized activations for SAEs; TCAV for concept attribution.
method.pretraining: The analyzed models used MMC (SleepFM), MAE (REVE), and MSP (LaBraM).

summary.one_liner: A mechanistic interpretability framework using Sparse Autoencoders to decompose EEG foundation model embeddings into clinically interpretable features.
summary.title: Mechanistic Interpretability of EEG Foundation Models via Sparse Autoencoders
summary.unique_contribution: A unified mechanistic interpretability pipeline for EEG foundation models that bridges high-dimensional embeddings to clinical physiology via SAEs, concept steering, and spectral decoding.

papers.title: Mechanistic Interpretability of EEG Foundation Models via Sparse Autoencoders

reasons: Explicitly targets and analyzes three specific EEG foundation models: SleepFM, REVE, and LaBraM.
Focuses on mechanistic interpretability and representational analysis of EEG-FMs.
Fits the ecosystem criteria by providing a framework for evaluating the internal computations and feature dictionaries of EEG-FMs.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces NeuroAtlas, a comprehensive benchmark spanning approximately 260k hours of EEG data across epilepsy, sleep medicine, brain age estimation, and BCI domains. It employs a unified frozen-encoder linear probing protocol to compare EEG-specific foundation models, general-purpose time-series models, and supervised baselines. The results demonstrate that EEG-specific pretraining does not consistently outperform generic time-series pretraining and that standard machine learning metrics often obscure real-world clinical utility.

headline_results: EEG-FMs do not consistently outperform TS-FMs
NeuroLM and REVE are the most consistent EEG-FMs
Standard ML metrics often fail to reflect clinical utility

key_points: Benchmark for EEG foundation models: Evaluates 20 models across 42 datasets and ~260k hours of data in four clinical domains.
Clinical metric focus: Utilizes domain-specific measures such as event-level sensitivity and hypnogram features, finding that standard AUROC and accuracy can be misleading.
Comparative analysis: Reveals that general-purpose time-series FMs perform on par with EEG-specific FMs, suggesting current models lack a truly unified, out-of-the-box capability.
limitations: Evaluation is restricted to linear probing, excluding fine-tuning and few-shot learning paradigms.
Certain promising models, such as BrainWave, were excluded due to unavailable implementations.
Brain-age gap (BAG) shows limited transfer for healthy-CI discrimination.
BCI performance is significantly influenced by non-neural confounds such as eye gaze.
summary.one_liner: A large-scale benchmark evaluating EEG and general time-series foundation models across 42 datasets and four clinical domains using clinically grounded metrics.
summary.title: NeuroAtlas: Benchmarking Foundation Models for Clinical EEG and Brain-Computer Interfaces
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces the largest EEG benchmark to date, incorporating 42 datasets and bespoke clinical evaluation metrics to expose the performance gap in current unified EEG foundation models.

papers.title: NeuroAtlas: Benchmarking Foundation Models for Clinical EEG and Brain-Computer Interfaces

reasons: Introduces NeuroAtlas, a large-scale benchmark specifically designed for evaluating EEG foundation models.
Evaluates both EEG-specific FMs and generic time-series FMs across 42 datasets.
Explicitly targets the EEG-FM ecosystem by providing datasets and clinical metrics for the development of unified EEG models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces handwriting decoding as a challenging motor task to evaluate EEG foundation models (FMs), arguing that typical motor imagery tasks are too coarse. The authors introduce the LOVW-8.5K dataset, designed to be free of oculomotor and temporal confounds found in prior work. Their results demonstrate that current FMs, including REVE and CBraMod, are outperformed by smaller specialist models like EEGNet, and that performance is heavily bottlenecked by movement-onset uncertainty and low single-trial SNR.

headline_results: EEGNet achieved 41.3% accuracy in movement-locked settings, significantly outperforming current FMs.

key_points: Benchmark for EEG foundation models: Proposes handwriting decoding as a more challenging motor task than coarse motor imagery classification to drive FM progress.
Performance gap: Finds that specialist models like EEGNet outperform current FMs (e.g., REVE, CBraMod) on the new LOVW-8.5K dataset.
Critical bottlenecks: Identifies movement-onset uncertainty and low single-trial SNR as primary obstacles, where increasing test-time SNR provides larger gains than scaling training data.
limitations: Small subject cohort (n=4) for the primary new dataset.
Limited number of letters (4) used for the classification task.
Cross-session stability for imagined handwriting (MI) was not significantly above chance.
High performance depends on movement-locked epoching, which is unrealistic for real-world BCI applications.

method.architecture: Comparison of specialist models (EEGNet, EEGConformer, SPaRCNet) and pretrained FMs (CBraMod, MIRepNet, REVE)
method.finetuning: Linear probing and full fine-tuning on the LOVW-8.5K dataset
method.pretraining: Evaluated existing FMs pretrained on large-scale EEG data

summary.one_liner: Proposes handwriting decoding as a rigorous benchmark for EEG foundation models, revealing that current FMs are outperformed by specialist models on fine-grained motor tasks.
summary.title: Handwriting decoding as a challenging motor task for EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a confound-free handwriting EEG dataset (LOVW-8.5K) and demonstrates that current EEG foundation models fail to outperform specialist models on fine-grained motor decoding.

papers.title: Handwriting decoding as a challenging motor task for EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly targets the evaluation of EEG Foundation Models (FMs) using handwriting decoding.
Proposes a new dataset and task specifically to benchmark and challenge the progress of EEG-FMs.
Analyzes the performance of existing EEG-FMs compared to task-specific models.

summary.detailed_summary: Unable to produce a reliable multi-sentence summary due to JSON validation failure.
key_points: unknown
unknown
unknown
limitations: unknown
summary_json_error
summary.one_liner: Summary unavailable due to JSON validation failure.
summary.title: Beyond Accuracy: Robustness, Interpretability and Expressiveness of EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: unknown

papers.title: Beyond Accuracy: Robustness, Interpretability and Expressiveness of EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly focuses on benchmarking and analyzing 'EEG foundation models (EEG-FMs)'
Evaluates six different EEG-FMs across eight datasets
Provides a systematic assessment of robustness, interpretability, and expressiveness specifically for EEG-FMs

summary.detailed_summary: PRiSE-EEG proposes a framework that balances shared EEG regularities with task-specific specialization through a depth-stratified Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformer. It utilizes a prior-guided continuous tokenizer that integrates static cortical/network priors and dynamic short-time channel interactions. The model's expert allocation is calibrated using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) from a pilot model to assign more shared experts to early layers and specialized experts to deeper layers. Experiments on 12 public benchmarks show significant performance gains, particularly in disease-related and cognitive tasks.

headline_results: 6-8% improvement on ADFTD and SEED-V
>9% AUROC gain on Things-EEG-2

key_points: New EEG foundation model: PRiSE-EEG combines prior-guided continuous tokenization with depth-stratified experts to learn reusable representations across heterogeneous EEG paradigms.
Method novelty: Uses CKA analysis of a pilot model to dynamically allocate shared and specialized experts across Transformer layers, following a general-to-specific transition.
Strong evidence: Outperforms existing EEG-FMs across 12 benchmarks, with primary metric improvements of 6-8% on complex tasks like ADFTD and SEED-V.
limitations: Model scale is modest compared to contemporary large-scale vision or language foundation models.
Inherent low signal-to-noise ratio of EEG signals necessitates further preprocessing advancements.
Lack of zero-shot or few-shot prompting capabilities; new tasks require retraining.
Currently focused on single-modality EEG and lacks integration with other modalities like fMRI or video.

method.architecture: Prior-guided continuous tokenizer and a depth-stratified MoE Transformer with interleaved temporal and group layers.
method.finetuning: Unified multi-dataset fine-tuning with dual-stream MLP heads.
method.objective: Masked temporal-patch reconstruction (MAE-TP), masked channel reconstruction (MAE-CH), and future-patch prediction (GPT).
method.pretraining: Self-supervised on 17 datasets using the three objectives + expert load-balancing loss.

summary.one_liner: An EEG foundation model using CKA-calibrated depth-stratified experts and prior-guided continuous tokenization to optimize cross-paradigm representation learning.
summary.title: PRiSE-EEG: A Prior-Guided Foundation Model with Depth-Stratified Experts for Cross-Paradigm EEG Representation Learning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a CKA-calibrated depth-stratified expert allocation mechanism that maps layer-wise representation sharedness to the proportion of shared versus specialized experts in an MoE Transformer.

papers.title: PRiSE-EEG: A Prior-Guided Foundation Model with Depth-Stratified Experts for Cross-Paradigm EEG Representation Learning

reasons: Explicitly proposes PRiSE-EEG, defined as an EEG foundation model.
Focuses on learning reusable representations across heterogeneous EEG paradigms.
Evaluated on 12 public EEG benchmarks to demonstrate cross-paradigm performance.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes Generative Visual Grounding (GVG), which uses an EEG-to-image model to hallucinate proxy images for both visually-evoked and non-visual clinical EEG. This approach maps neural signals into the discrete visual token space of MLLMs (X-Omni and Janus), bypassing the information loss associated with pure text alignment. Experiments across six benchmarks show that trimodal (Image+Text) alignment outperforms single-modality variants and remains competitive with larger text-aligned models like NeuroLM.

headline_results: GVG-Janus (Trimodal) is the strongest multitask model
GVG-X-Omni matches NeuroLM-XL on SEED with 10x fewer trainable parameters

key_points: New EEG foundation model: GVG translates brainwaves into discrete visual tokens, allowing frozen MLLMs to interpret neural dynamics using pre-trained visual priors.
Method novelty: Employs a generative 'visual translator' to create proxy images for clinical EEG, enabling a unified visual-alignment pipeline for 'blind' datasets.
Strong evidence: GVG-X-Omni matches a 1.7B text-aligned baseline on SEED with 10x fewer trainable parameters.
limitations: Synthesized proxy images lack high-frequency micro-details.
Reconstruction is a coarse visual summary rather than a literal physical reconstruction of internal percepts.
Reliance on the quality of the AVDE generative model for clinical proxy images.
High memory use for GVG-X-Omni due to the 7B frozen backbone.

papers.title: DARE-EEG: A Foundation Model for Mining Dual-Aligned Representation of EEG

reasons: Explicitly identifies DARE-EEG as a self-supervised foundation model for EEG.
Focuses on pre-training on large-scale EEG data to learn generalizable representations for diverse BCI applications.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces a universal microstate tokenizer created by clustering continuous EEG signals from a large medical dataset into discrete microstates using streaming k-means. This representation is then applied to sleep staging, emotion recognition, and motor imagery classification, consistently outperforming traditional time- and frequency-domain features. The authors demonstrate that microstates are more scalable with data size and provide biologically interpretable links to cognitive states.

headline_results: Microstates achieved highest accuracy (e.g., 0.81 in sleep staging) and showed better scalability than time/frequency features.

key_points: New EEG representation method: leverages biologically grounded microstates as discrete tokens to bridge deep learning and neural activity patterns.
Demonstrates superior performance over raw and STFT features across sleep staging, emotion recognition, and motor imagery tasks.
Shows that microstate representations are more scalable with increasing data size and offer higher interpretability regarding brain activity patterns.
limitations: Tokenizer training was performed only on sleep data.
Evaluated on a limited number of tasks and datasets.
Focused primarily on the representation side rather than developing a full pre-trained model.

method.architecture: Tokenizer based on streaming k-means clustering of Global Field Power (GFP) peaks.
method.finetuning: Downstream classification using CNN+LSTM, Sleep Transformer, and ResNet.
method.objective: Unsupervised clustering into k=1000 discrete microstates.
method.pretraining: Fitted on the Human Sleep Project (HSP) dataset.

summary.one_liner: Proposes a universal EEG tokenizer based on biologically grounded microstates to improve performance and interpretability across diverse downstream tasks.
summary.title: Atoms of Thought: Universal EEG Representation Learning with Microstates
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a universal EEG tokenizer based on biologically grounded microstates that generalizes across diverse cognitive tasks and outperforms conventional time-frequency features.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes MSCGC-KAN, a specialized classification head designed to be attached to the pre-trained CBraMod backbone for EEG emotion recognition. It introduces a multi-scale causal residual graph convolution module to capture temporal dynamics and channel connectivity, alongside a Kolmogorov-Arnold-inspired layer for nonlinear feature mapping. Evaluation on the FACED and SEED-VII datasets demonstrates that this structured head significantly outperforms simple linear projections, improving balanced accuracy by up to 5.91 percentage points.

headline_results: Balanced Accuracy of 60.66% on FACED
Balanced Accuracy of 33.27% on SEED-VII
Outperformed CBraMod+Linear baseline by 5.91% and 2.03% respectively

key_points: New EEG emotion recognition method: Enhances pre-trained foundation models (CBraMod) using a structured task head instead of a simple linear classifier.
Combines multi-scale causal convolutions for temporal dynamics with learnable graph convolutions for inter-channel functional connectivity.
Utilizes a KAN-inspired analytic basis expansion layer to improve nonlinear discriminative power, achieving 60.66% balanced accuracy on the FACED dataset.
limitations: Evaluated on only two public emotion datasets, requiring further validation for broader generalization.
Convolution kernel sizes and analytic basis functions are experience-driven rather than optimized via search.
Neurophysiological meaning of the learnable adjacency matrix requires deeper investigation through statistical tests.
Lacks incorporation of hemispheric asymmetry or cross-patch temporal attention.

method.architecture: Pre-trained CBraMod backbone followed by a task head consisting of MCRBlock-GCN (parallel causal Conv1D branches and a learnable GCN adjacency matrix) and a KANLinear layer (analytic basis expansion using h, h^2, sin(h), and tanh(h)).
method.finetuning: Differential learning rates (1:5 ratio between backbone and head) with cosine annealing and gradient clipping.

summary.one_liner: A structured task head combining multi-scale causal graph convolutions and KAN-inspired nonlinear mapping to enhance the fine-tuning of pre-trained EEG foundation models for emotion recognition.
summary.title: MSCGC-KAN: Multi-scale Causal Graph Convolution and Kolmogorov-Arnold Feature Mapping for EEG Emotion Recognition
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a structured task-head framework that integrates multi-scale causal temporal modeling, learnable graph-based spatial connectivity, and KAN-inspired nonlinear mapping for the downstream adaptation of EEG foundation models.

summary.title: EEG-FM-Audit: A Systematic Evaluation and Analysis Pipeline for EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: unknown

papers.title: EEG-FM-Audit: A Systematic Evaluation and Analysis Pipeline for EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly targets the systematic evaluation and analysis of EEG Foundation Models (FMs) in both title and abstract.
Proposes a dedicated pipeline (EEG-FM-Audit) for benchmarking and probing EEG-FMs.
Evaluates four state-of-the-art EEG-FMs to analyze their effectiveness and neurophysiological properties.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a multi-dimensional evaluation framework to assess the generalization of EEG foundation models under realistic low-resource constraints. It evaluates models across three axes: parameter efficiency (using linear probing and LoRA), sample efficiency (varying label budgets), and channel efficiency (using sparse and lobe-restricted montages). By testing models like LaBraM, CSBrain, and CBraMod across six diverse datasets, the authors demonstrate that while foundation models significantly outperform supervised baselines in long-context tasks like sleep staging, they offer limited gains or even underperform in short-window BCI tasks. The results suggest that current coarse tokenization strategies may hinder the capture of fine-grained neural dynamics.

headline_results: FMs outperform supervised models in long-context tasks (e.g., Sleep EDF).
FMs show comparable or inferior performance in short-window BCI tasks.
FMs exhibit limited robustness to channel-constrained settings.

key_points: Benchmark framework: Proposes a multi-dimensional evaluation of EEG foundation models focusing on parameter, sample, and channel efficiency.
Task-dependent utility: Foundation models excel in long-context clinical tasks but are comparable to small supervised models in short-window BCI tasks.
Robustness gaps: Current EEG-FMs show limited robustness to reduced channel counts and coarse tokenization may limit effectiveness for transient responses.
limitations: Focuses primarily on masked reconstruction-based foundation models.
Does not evaluate models trained under identical settings to isolate specific pre-training axes.
Limited range of tasks and recording conditions for sample efficiency analysis.
Interaction between inter-subject and intra-subject variability in low-resource settings remains unexplored.

method.finetuning: Downstream task adaptation via linear probing and LoRA
method.objective: Evaluation of pre-trained models using Linear Probing and PEFT (LoRA)
method.pretraining: Masked reconstruction on large-scale EEG corpora

summary.one_liner: A multi-dimensional framework to evaluate EEG foundation models across parameter, sample, and channel efficiency axes.
summary.title: A Multi-dimensional Framework for Evaluating Generalization in EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a systematic evaluation protocol to assess EEG foundation models under realistic low-resource constraints, revealing a performance gap between long-context and short-window tasks.
